meta.title: S210 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes E-Class S210 engine — M111, M112, M113, OM604, OM605, OM606, OM611. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and E 55 AMG explained. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes E-Class S210 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S210 engine typically costs £1,300–£9,000 supply only, depending on engine — the volume OM604 diesel sits at £1,300–£2,800, while the M113 V8 in the E 55 T AMG is the most expensive at £5,000–£9,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,300 depending on engine complexity and specialist rates. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes E-Class S210 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM606 3.0-litre straight-six diesel (E 300 Turbodiesel, 177 PS) is widely considered one of the most reliable engines Mercedes ever made — legendary over-engineering, mechanical injection, and 300,000+ mile potential. The M112 3.2-litre V6 (E 320 T, 224 PS) is the best petrol option — smooth, refined, and introduced with significant weight savings over the M104 straight-six.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes E-Class S210?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cooling system leaks on the M112 V6 and M113 V8, glow plug and injector wear on the OM60x diesels, biodegradable wiring harness degradation on pre-1997 M111 cars, and rear self-levelling suspension failure on high-mileage estates. The 1997 engine modernisation addressed many issues, but cooling system neglect remains the primary cause of failure.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes E-Class S210 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Overall reliability is excellent for a late-1990s Mercedes — particularly the diesel straight-six OM606, which is legendary. The M112 V6 is also highly durable. The main caveats are the pre-1997 biodegradable wiring harness (budget for replacement) and cooling system neglect on the V6 and V8 — both are well-understood and avoidable. Post-1997 cars are much improved.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes E-Class S210 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM604 diesel (E 200 D, E 220 D) is underpowered and suffers from indirect-injection drawbacks — we'd recommend the OM605 or OM606 instead. Also avoid any pre-1997 petrol car without documented wiring harness replacement — chasing electrical gremlins can be a costly nightmare. The E 55 T AMG is a genuine modern classic but carries eye-watering maintenance bills — only buy one with deep pockets and full service history.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my S210 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Persistent coolant loss or overheating (M112/M113 cooling system), poor cold starting or excessive smoke on diesels (glow plug/injector wear), intermittent misfires and electrical gremlins (wiring harness on pre-1997 M111), or a loud clunking from the rear (self-levelling suspension failure) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes E-Class S210 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £6,000 E 320 T, a £2,500–£4,500 recon is 42–75% of the car's value — borderline but viable. Against a tired £2,000 E 200 T, a £1,500–£2,800 recon is 75–140% — walk away. For an E 55 T AMG, a £25,000+ car justifies a £5,000–£9,000 recon; a £15,000 example makes it a much harder call. Our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold is the line.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes E-Class S210 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM606 straight-six diesel can exceed 300,000 miles with proper maintenance and prompt glow plug/injector attention. The M112 V6 can reach 200,000+ miles with regular servicing and cooling system care. The M111 four-cylinder petrols are similarly durable once the wiring harness is addressed. The OM611 CDI is also proven, though injector wear is common at higher mileages.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the S210 and W210 E-Class?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both are the second-generation E-Class (1995–2003 for the saloon, 1996–2002 for the estate). The W210 is the 4-door saloon (1995–2003). The S210 is the 5-door estate (1996–2002, this page). Engines are shared across both body styles, though the estate adds rear self-levelling suspension and additional structural reinforcement, which affects weight and towing capacity.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes E-Class S210 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (under the bonnet or on the driver's door pillar) and on the engine block itself. Tell us your variant (E 200 T, E 230 T, E 200 T Kompressor, E 240 T, E 320 T, E 430 T, E 55 T AMG, E 200 D, E 220 D, E 220 CDI, E 250 D, E 250 Turbodiesel, E 290 Turbodiesel, E 300 Turbodiesel) and approximate model year and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes E-Class S210 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the second-generation E-Class Estate — from the M111 four-cylinder petrol to the M112 V6, the M113 V8, and the OM606 diesel-six that defined Mercedes' load-lugging legend. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the 1997 engine modernisation explained. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [PATTERN DATA].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes E-Class S210 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the S210 estate specifically — including the full M111 petrol four-cylinder range, the M112 V6, the M113 V8, and the comprehensive diesel line-up from four-cylinder to six-cylinder.

overview.intro: The S210 is the estate variant of the second-generation Mercedes-Benz E-Class, produced from 1996 to 2003 as the successor to the S124. It shared the W210 saloon's distinctive four-headlight design and introduced a more spacious interior with a 70-litre increase in cargo volume. The engine range spanned M111 four-cylinder petrols, the M112 V6 introduced in 1997, M113 V8s in the E 430 and E 55 AMG, plus OM604, OM605, OM606 and OM611 diesel units. A major engine modernisation in 1997 brought the M112 V6 and updated four-cylinder engines.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W210/S210 (second generation) • Predecessor: Mercedes-Benz E-Class Estate (S124) • Successor: Mercedes-Benz E-Class Estate (S211) • Sibling body styles in this generation: W210 saloon (1995–2003).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The S210 E-Class Estate generated UK engine enquiries in 2025 [PATTERN DATA], with the M112 V6 petrol and OM606 diesel six remaining the most commonly discussed among specialists.

items.quote: Thin-Data Fallback mode — one paragraph: The S210's most dependable engine is the OM606 3.0-litre straight-six diesel (E 300 Turbodiesel, 177 PS) — legendary Mercedes over-engineering with mechanical injection, proven durability, and 300,000+ mile potential. The M112 3.2-litre V6 (E 320, 224 PS) is the best petrol option — smooth, refined, and introduced in 1997 with significant weight savings over the M104 straight-six. The M113 V8 in the E 430 (279 PS) offers effortless V8 performance but carries higher running costs, while the E 55 AMG (354 PS) is a genuine modern classic — epic V8 soundtrack but eye-watering maintenance bills. Avoid early OM604 diesels — they are underpowered and suffer from indirect-injection drawbacks. Also avoid any car with neglected cooling systems, as the M112 and M113 V8s are sensitive to overheating, and pre-1997 cars may suffer from biodegradable wiring harness issues.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Budget Choice — simplest S210 to run
Supercharged — more power, more to maintain
Reliable V6 — the 170 PS estate workhorse
Best Petrol — 224 PS V6, proven durability
V8 Power — effortless but costly to maintain
AMG Icon — epic engine, epic bills

economicsRule.title: The S210 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,000–£4,500 recon on a sub-£3,000 S210 sits at 67–150% of the car's value — at that point, scrap value usually wins. Against a well-kept £6,000+ example, the same recon drops to 33–75% — viable. For an E 55 AMG, a £25,000+ car justifies a £5,000–£9,000 recon; a £15,000 example makes it a much harder call. Our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold is the line — value the car first, then the engine.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S210 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 200 T (M111.942/945) — simplest engine, cheapest parts, no turbo or supercharger complexity.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S210 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 55 T AMG (M113.980) — V8 complexity, specialist-only work, eye-watering parts prices.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S210 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: E 300 Turbodiesel (OM606.962) — legendary six-cylinder diesel, 177 PS, 40+ MPG, bulletproof reliability.

problems.title: M112 & M113 V8 Cooling System Leaks
problems.affectedModels: S210 E 240 T, E 320 T, E 430 T, E 55 T AMG (all M112 and M113 petrol variants).
problems.rootCause: The M112 V6 and M113 V8's cooling systems — particularly the water pump, thermostat housing, and coolant crossover pipes — are prone to leaks. The V6 and V8 layouts make access tight, increasing labour costs. The M112 was introduced in 1997 with lightweight materials and three-valve technology, but cooling system neglect remains the primary cause of engine failure.

tieredCosts.work: Replace water pump, thermostat, and coolant pipes
Full cooling system overhaul + engine inspection if overheated

problems.recommendation: Check coolant levels regularly and look for signs of leaks around the water pump and thermostat housing — £500–£900 in preventative work is cheap insurance against the £2,000+ bill that follows an overheat. This is the single most important job on any M112 or M113-powered S210.

cta.label: Compare M112/M113 engine prices for your S210 E-Class →

problems.title: OM606 Diesel Glow Plug & Injector Issues
problems.affectedModels: S210 E 300 Turbodiesel (OM606.962) and other OM60x diesel variants.
problems.rootCause: The OM606 straight-six diesel uses indirect injection with pre-combustion chambers. Glow plugs wear out over time, causing poor cold starting and increased smoke. Injector seals can leak, causing fuel contamination of the engine oil. The OM606 is legendary for durability — 300,000+ miles is achievable — but the glow plug and injector system requires attention.

tieredCosts.work: Replace glow plugs and inspect injectors
Full injector replacement and fuel system flush

problems.recommendation: If your S210 diesel is hard to start when cold, have the glow plugs tested immediately — £200–£400 is cheap insurance against the £1,000+ bill that follows if failed glow plugs cause injector damage or hydraulic lock.

cta.label: Compare OM606 engine prices for your S210 diesel →

problems.title: Pre-1997 M111 Wiring Harness Degradation
problems.affectedModels: S210 E 200 T, E 230 T (pre-1997 M111 petrol variants).
problems.rootCause: Early S210s used biodegradable wiring insulation that degrades with age and heat, causing short circuits, misfires, and erratic engine behaviour — the same issue that affects other 1990s Mercedes models.
problems.recommendation: If your pre-1997 S210 has no documented wiring harness replacement, budget for it immediately — £400–£700 is cheap insurance against the £1,000+ bill for chasing intermittent electrical faults. Post-1997 cars are much improved.

cta.label: Compare M111 wiring harness replacement quotes for your S210 E-Class →

problems.title: S210 Rear Air Suspension & Self-Levelling System Failure
problems.affectedModels: All S210 estate variants, particularly high-mileage examples.
problems.rootCause: The S210's rear self-levelling suspension (standard on estate models) uses hydraulic components that can fail over time — rear shock absorbers, accumulators, and the hydraulic pump are common failure points. While not an engine failure, it's one of the most common S210 enquiries and a significant ownership cost.

tieredCosts.work: Replace accumulators and check hydraulic fluid
Full rear suspension overhaul including shocks and pump

problems.recommendation: Have the rear self-levelling suspension inspected at every service after 80,000 miles — £500–£800 in preventative work is a fraction of the £1,200+ bill if the system fails completely and damages the pump or lines.

cta.label: Compare S210 self-levelling suspension repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: S210 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are S210 estate only. W210 saloon shares most of these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page, since body style affects weight, aerodynamics, and to some extent, labour access. The S210 was produced from May 1995 to December 2002.
